info: A longer, warmer interstadial saw birch-pine woodland spread widely, with hazel making its first appearance — the most significant vegetation shift since the LGM.
background: A longer and warmer interstadial that saw the most significant vegetation development since the LGM. Birch woodland spread widely across central and northern Europe, with Scots pine expanding from eastern refugia and beginning to mix with birch across much of the map area. Hazel appeared for the first time in the pollen record in some areas, a sign of warming and a disturbance-adapted pioneer responding to browse pressure. The landscape was an open birch-pine woodland — not dense forest, but a mosaic of wooded patches, scrub and open ground, probably comparable structurally to present-day Scandinavian birch woodland zones. Open ground remained extensive, perhaps 50–60% of the landscape. Fire, increasingly of human origin, began to play a role alongside the diminishing large-herbivore influence in maintaining open patches.
